item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It wasn't until I read your interpretation that I could put the pieces together. I think the final dialogue could be edited to be a bit more clear. But the rest is pretty great. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your reading ImperiumRomanun.

+ About the article
- an explain

In my original idea, the phenomenon was due to a reality restructuring event. In the original timeline, there was a researcher named Andres Bolivar who, due to actions beyond his duties, died during a containment gap. However, "something" (whether called Death, Fate or some other higher force) decided that he should not die on that occasion, giving him a second chance, this new life being, however, much less remarkable than the previous one. There is a problem and it is that there are still "echoes" of Bolívar's actions, memories of those who either witnessed or heard about his actions, and in the face of the incongruity that is generated, that same force that gave him a second chance is forced to correct that "failure". First, it seeks to distort Bolivar's memories, so that he is forgotten, and since this is not effective, it eliminates the subjects who have the most memories (who are the ones who initiate the dissemination of history) and induces amnesia in those who heard it, in the period corresponding to when the incident occurred (similar to a computer registry cleanup)

As the Foundation does not know these antecedents, it takes the phenomenon as a conventional memetic infection, especially virulent and transmissible through the story, with certain dangerous cognitive elements, which is why it takes the measures it considers appropriate.

However, the interpretation changes when Bolivien appears, who shares the characteristics described for Bolívar, and there the questions arise: What is his relationship with the phenomenon? Is he perhaps Bolivar? Why is he there?

The differences in the name of the Junior Researcher and the Class D are due to the way memory works, it tends to be inaccurate, and at the same time to the language differences of the staff of Site-34 (they are Spanish speaking, so the name and surname sounded like "Andrés Bolívar")

One interpretation given by several readers that I liked a lot is that the phenomenon is due to the unfulfilled dreams and aspirations of Bolivien, who by becoming a Class D without hope of freedom or a better life, dreams of a new life in a faraway place, far from crime and violence, being someone respectable. And because as a child he wanted to be a scientist, that form took on its aspiration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure what to think of this.

The whole "SCP that only affects the foundation/employees" idea has lost its original flavor to me. Nothing wrong with that though, it's just another way of writing a skip.

But why is SCP-5030 just a random D-Class with ADHD? The interview didn't seem to clear things up, either. If he showed some indecisiveness, like if he suddenly decided that he loved the atmosphere of the room after a minute or two, then it might help clear things up with the reader, since his personality would fit his anomalous properties.

And why does it kill people? I'm unsure if it adds any substance to the skip. The foundation would contain it regardless. I could see it working if the Chaos Insurgency or some other hostile GOI were responsible somehow, but I don't think it fits well with the skip you're going for.

Just going to abstain from voting for now. This is my first post, so my judgement is probably off anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that pic of scp-6030 at the end scares me. seriously not joking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I corrected the missed accent marks in Bolívar and Martínez names, thanks for noticing it.

About the final interview, it can be considered either as an explanation or a hint, I thought that with only the description and the interviews of the affected subjects the article could be confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dialogue in the final interview leaves something to be desired, which is unfortunate, as it left me with a poor final impression. I also noticed you dropped the accent marks in both of Bolívar's names toward the end of the article. Interesting core idea, though?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much for the revision, I will ask if I can edit the article during the voting period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Again, I like your ideas, and I hope you keep writing :)

As you've indicated a desire for spelling/grammar feedback for English, I put some in a block here. My main feedback is that in a formal or technical writing style (like the SCP Foundation entries) English is surprisingly strict about how to use comparisons.

+ show block
– hide block

First para:

Those affected by the treatment should be observed for for a minimum of 60 days.

This should probably be "exposed to the anomaly" rather than "affected by the treatment". As written this would suggest observing people who are successfully treated, but not observing people who are /not/ successfully treated.

Second para:

of the Foundation for mentions and references about SCP-5000

Perhaps overly strict, but this should be: "mentions of and references to" — these prepositions agree with the verb form of the noun, so you 'refer to' things. I am sorry that "mentions of" doesn't follow the rule I'm claiming exists here!

Adding, those responsible for these references

"Adding" should be "Additionally" or "Furthermore". There's some unintentional ambiguity in "those responsible", as "responsible" can mean "supposed to be in charge of", "actually in charge of", or "the author of". I think you mean "the authors of these references".

Third paragraph: Rereading this paragraph I /really/ like the efficient project management the Foundation is deploying here — deploying weak amnestics, estimating the needed innoculation for immunity, and giving a timeline for completion. This is really nice detail. No suggested fixes here.

Fourth para:

SCP-5000 corresponds to a memetic phenomenon that has been described as affecting only employees of the Foundation.

"Described as" suggests there is someone else doing the describing, but this is the documentation for SCP-5000, so this is confusing. Similarly, "Corresponds to" suggests there's some system being compared to the Foundation database e.g. the Global Occult Initiative's database. I suggest "SCP-5000 is a memetic phenomenon that has only been observed to affect employees of the Foundation".

Still fourth para:

This phenomenon is characterized both by the induction of anomalous memories in those affected and by retroactive alteration of the local registration systems, making them consistent with what was indicated by those affected, regardless of whether this alteration contradicts data in the public domain or information described in other registration systems.

This sentence is a long chain of relative clauses that require the reader to keep several nested comparisons in mind while reading it. As Andrés Bolívar has not been introduced yet, "local registration systems" doesn't have the context needed to make it clear that it's personnel databases that are affected. I would split this after the comma to make two shorter sentences, each simpler to read and maybe change "registration systems" to "personnel databases". Lots of things can have registrations (e.g. cars) but only people are in personnel databases.

Fifth para:

Even if there is a certain level of divergence in the details, the accounts tend to agree in their general aspects, even among subjects who have not had direct communication or contact with each other.

The word "Even" here is again making a comparison against something which isn't present. This would be fine in informal writing as it's implied you're comparing to 'normality', but is unusual in technical writing where it is important that the reader get a very precise understanding.

Sixth para:

Inspections of the registry systems of sectors of Site-34 not affected by the phenomenon, as well as of external registry systems and ARCA (Anomalous Contingency Rescue Archive), have made it possible to conclude that Junior Researcher Bolívar is not a current or historic staff member and no events described by SCP-5000 affected personnel occurred at any Foundation Site during December 2018.

This sentence has "subject confusion". Let's split this up into two, then look at the first half in more detail:

Inspections of the registry systems of sectors of Site-34 not affected by the phenomenon, as well as of external registry systems and ARCA (Anomalous Contingency Rescue Archive).

The comma after 'phenomenon' here reads like a list, so it sounds like the sentence is saying "Inspections aren't affected by the phenomenon, and inspections aren't affected by external registry systems either". When you have a list of nouns like this it's sensible to keep them close to each other in the sentence. However, here the nouns are quite long, so that doesn't seem to be a good option. Splitting a sentence into shorter sentences is always a good choice, so I suggest "Inspection of the Site 34 registry systems are not affected by the phenomenon. External registry systems and ARCA (Anomalous Contingency Rescue Archive) are also unaffected."

These inspections have made it possible to conclude that Junior Researcher Bolívar is not a current or historic staff member and no events described by SCP-5000 affected personnel occurred at any Foundation Site during December 2018.

After the split, the second half of this sentence is fine. By adding two splits in this sentence you get three sentences that are both simpler.

I've reviewed the main text for main points of spelling and grammar but not the collapsibles. I hope it's helpful, but please let me know if I've failed in that at any point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s an interesting explain to the article but originally I thought the SCP in another way.

Generally, I like the articles stand alone and when the anomalies are manageable, because above all, in universe the Foundation's staff is highly qualified, so they would be expected to respond in the best possible way.

+ An explain
– hide block

I preferred to leave certain ambiguous elements in the article, both because of the element of mystery associated with the contest and because of personal taste. I like articles that can be interpreted in different ways by the reader.

What the Foundation initially takes, based on the evidence it has, as a memetic infection, turns out to be something else. There is certainly a memetic element, the anomalous origin of memories, but those memories have a tangible origin. In one version of reality, Bolívar/Bolivien is a researcher at the Foundation and dies as a result. However, something decides to give him a second chance, but in exchange for living a much less remarkable life, and that is the life Bolivien has. For more details, please see the answer to ModernMajorGeneral.

Secondly; there were some minor spelling/grammar issues that broke the SCP tone for me; a look over by a kind editor would fix this.

This is because I am not a native speaker of English, and after the translation and even after the proofreading some details and expressions may sound strange. I would appreciate it if someone could point out these problems so that the necessary corrections can be made if corrections are allowed during the voting period.

Thirdly; the reveal of the person being real seemed unrelated to the story so far (except by name) so lessening the tension. Is this person a victim? A saboteur? A result of the virus? Any of these could work, but at the moment I can't tell which it's meant to be.

Bolivien's presence indicates that there are more than changing memories, that something in reality itself was altered and that certain echoes persist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Leaning downvote — but a good start!

Rationale:

  • First; I like the idea! A memetic hazard that is too harmful to the hosts for it to spread too quickly, as it's both self-destructive and kills the hosts in short order. If this were the point of the SCP, I'd have upvoted. It's nice that there's SCPs being written that /are/ manageable; a lot of more recent SCPs seem to have the Foundation constantly one step away from destruction.
  • Secondly; there were some minor spelling/grammar issues that broke the SCP tone for me; a look over by a kind editor would fix this.
  • Thirdly; the reveal of the person being real seemed unrelated to the story so far (except by name) so lessening the tension. Is this person a victim? A saboteur? A result of the virus? Any of these could work, but at the moment I can't tell which it's meant to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The language was a bit awkward, and I didn't really feel the payoff. I don't know, it just felt a bit flat for me, and i get there has to be a mystery, but the ending with the d class didn't really tie into it in anyways, just felt disconnected. Either way, great effort, +1 for that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I glad you like the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this article. The language was a little awkward at times, I see in your above comment that English isn't your first language, this is understandable. But the idea is really cool and the ending is interesting. My theory is that the memories of Bolivar are from alternate universes where he worked for the Foundation. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can get behind the explanation of the beliefs of him as a heroic saviour being some kind of mental projection of his hopes and dreams, if that's what you were going for, but in that case I'm not sure how the second stage of infection fits into this.

I like that explanation, but as you mentioned it is inconsistent with Stage II and therefore would not be correct.

I had preferred to leave a certain element of ambiguity in the article both because of the element of mystery associated with the competition and because of a theme of personal taste, I like articles that can give rise to different explanations by the reader.

+ Spoilers
- or an explain?

What occurs in this story is an event of reconfiguration of reality, with the Class D subject interviewed being the center of that entire event. In the original timeline, Andrew Bolivien becomes a researcher hired by the Foundation, dying during a Containment Breach. But someone or something, whether called the Death, the Fate or the Reality, decides to give him a second chance, and consequently live a new life. However, this life is less noticeable and less successful, has more obstacles, never becomes a scientist, that is, is a "random guy" as you describe. The problem is that there are subjects who somehow have memories associated with Bolivien and his actions in the original timeline, and how those are remarkable memories, they share them with others. What is the solution? To distort those memories, so that the subjects cannot remember Bolivien on good terms. However, they continue to share them, the solution is to eliminate them, at least those who have the most memories (certain Alpha subjects) (a sort of "purge the data record of reality") and erase the memory of those who have the fewest memories (the Beta) because they received the story second hand.

Consequently, what the Foundation takes to be a memetic infection can be interpreted more like echoes of a restructuring event. The members of Site-34 speak Spanish, so they interpret the surname "Bolivien" as "Bolívar," although it also works in the opposite sense, that is, in his new life, this subject is surnamed Bolivien, being his original surname Bolívar.

I also found some of the dialogue stilted and there were a few phrasing errors throughout (eg 'Have been described two classes of infected').

This is because I am not a native speaker of English, therefore after translation and even after proofreading there may be some expressions that sound strange. I will correct those details as I find them and hopefully avoid them in future works

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea here, but unless I'm missing something, there doesn't seem to be much of a payoff. The mystery of who this guy is, or why people have these memories of him, just ends with him being just some random guy.

I can get behind the explanation of the beliefs of him as a heroic saviour being some kind of mental projection of his hopes and dreams, if that's what you were going for, but in that case I'm not sure how the second stage of infection fits into this.

I also found some of the dialogue stilted and there were a few phrasing errors throughout (eg 'Have been described two classes of infected').

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
My special thanks to cybersqyd for the helpful feedback given for this article, both in SPaG and in thematic and narrative.

I usually don't write about mystery, but I wanted to try.

It’s a story about memories and what believes that is true. I hope you enjoy this article, maybe it has several interpretations…

Numbers: 5050, 5030, 5042, 5550, 5432

Image Sources:

The first image was created by me.

The second image is from here and its complies with license requirements.
